formatted_human_prompt:
Analyze the following database requirement. You MUST follow the 5-step reasoning process
defined in the system prompt. Show your complete thinking for each step, then output the JSON.

Database requirement:
ER Modeling Scenario
Integrated Hospital Management System for Multiple Facilities
Business Context
You have been hired to model the database for a hospital network that operates 15 units across 3 Brazilian states. The system must control, record and monitor the entry and exit of all people who access the hospitals, including staff, visitors and patients, ensuring traceability, security and compliance with the institution's internal rules.
Functional Requirements
1. Hospital
Each hospital has departments responsible for specific activities. Not every facility in the network needs to offer all services: each hospital declares the departments, human resources and capacities it has (available teams and departments).
Departments present in the network:
* Emergency
* ICU (Intensive Care Unit)
* Surgical Center / Operating Theaters
* Adult Ward
* Pediatric Ward
* Maternity
* Radiology / Imaging (X-ray, CT, MRI)
* Clinical Laboratory
* Outpatient Clinic
* Hospital Pharmacy
* Social Services
* Triage
* Reception
1.1 Departments
* Each department has an assigned coordinator.
* There must be a leader or person responsible for the department in every shift (there should always be someone accountable for the department at any time).

2. People Management
The hospital receives individuals in multiple categories; some are employees and others are not. Categories include: visitor, patient, physician, nurse, resident physician, researcher and administrative staff. Physicians may work in multiple units under different contract types (for example, CLT, PJ, on-call, daily-rate). Residents are licensed physicians in training who also attend patients under supervision, so it is important to record the supervising physician for each resident. VIP patients have special care rules and enhanced privacy (these are procedural and privacy measures, not related to the medical quality of care). Employees may have dependents who are also patients. The hospital records the following for every person who accesses it: full name, addresses, contact phone numbers, CPF, RG and date of birth. (CPF and RG are Brazilian identifiers: CPF = taxpayer ID, RG = identity card.)
2.1 Patients
   * Each patient receives an identification wristband.
   * The system must record patient movements between departments, including hospital admission, transfers between departments and discharge.
   * A complete history of the departments each patient has passed through must be maintained.
   * The system must indicate whether the patient is awaiting care, in care, admitted (inpatient), under observation, in surgery, or discharged.
2.2 Visitors
   * Not every visitor is accompanying a patient.
   * The type of visitor must be recorded (for example, technical visit, regulatory inspection, etc.).
   * Visitor classification is essential to determine access restrictions (important information for the security team).
   * Each visitor receives an identification card at entry.
   * Not every patient is allowed to have a companion. Valid companion cases include: minors; elderly patients; pregnant patients; women in labor; postpartum patients; patients with prolonged or severe hospitalization, at the discretion of the attending physician or care team; and situations of emotional or cognitive vulnerability when the physician or social services deem it necessary.
   * If patient isolation is required, the physician must document the reason for isolation in a clinical note.
   * The system must record the visitor's exit (mandatory).
   * Access outside visiting hours must be prevented unless special authorization is granted by the hospital.
2.3 Employees
   * Employees must be clearly identified.
   * A researcher can be either a nurse or a physician only; researcher is not an independent occupational category.
   * A resident physician cannot be a coordinator or be responsible for a department, even temporarily.
   * Physicians and nurses may also hold administrative roles (for example, responsible for coordinating a department).
   * Any employee may still be treated by the hospital, be present as a visitor, or accompany a patient.
2.4 Addresses
   * A person may have more than one registered address.
   * Employees update their addresses periodically.
   * When possible, patients and visitors should also have updated addresses.
   * It is important to store the timestamp of the last address update.
   * If the last update was more than six months ago, the system should alert and request a registration update.
2.5 Identification Cards
   * Identification cards have an issue date and an expiry date, recorded in the system for security purposes.
   * Cards have a type that designates access level (visually distinguished by different colors).
   * When a card is in use it is marked as active.
   * Because patients and visitors do not have personalized cards, a card's identifier number may be temporarily linked to the person's record while in use.
   * Only personalized cards (employees) must include a photo.
3. Employee Scheduling
Hospital units require an integrated time and attendance system that covers all 15 units to prevent staff from being accidentally scheduled at two locations at the same time or assigned impractical schedules. Professionals follow different work routines. Administration must precisely control the hours worked by each employee. Different hospitals in the network may have different scheduling contracts with the same employee. It is also necessary to record events that remove an employee from duties, such as days off, medical leave, vacation or training.
3.1 Time Clock Records
   * The system must record each employee's clock-in and clock-out times.
   * As mentioned, each employee has an identification card, which facilitates this task.
   * Time records may generate an alert if an employee exceeds scheduled hours or misses a clock record; this is useful for HR.
3.2 Work Shifts
   * Some employees have more flexible shifts than others.
   * Administrative staff generally have fixed weekdays and start/end times.
   * Nurses may have fixed rules or work rotating shifts.
   * Physicians similarly may have variable schedules and may come to the hospital only to perform consultations, monitoring, surgeries, etc., leaving when those activities are completed.
   * The employment contract governs how working hours are administered by the contracting institution.
   * The key requirement is accurate recording of clock-in and clock-out times for HR verification of compliance with agreed schedules.
3.3 Availability Exceptions
   * Reasons that remove a professional from scheduled activities must be well documented.
   * Each exception should include start and end dates, type (day off, medical leave, vacation, training) and an optional textual note.
3.4 Shift Swaps
   * Shift swaps among health staff are common and permitted.
   * Whenever a swap occurs, it is important to record the involved employees and the coordinator who approved the swap.
   * Each department has its coordinator, who may be a physician, a nurse or an administrative employee.
   * The justification for the swap request should also be recorded (useful for HR metrics about the need for permanent schedule changes, employee health considerations, etc.).
4. Special Situations and Contingency Procedures
4.1 Lost Identification Card
The system must allow immediate registration of a lost card. When a loss is reported:
   * the card is automatically invalidated;
   * a new card may be issued and linked to the person;
   * every access attempt using the lost card is blocked and logged;
   * the event generates an alert for security.
A history of cards (active, replaced, lost, expired) must be maintained.
4.2 Card Failure (Defective or Inoperable Card)
When a card fails to read, the system must record: the person involved; the reason for the failure; the read attempt; and the manual verification performed (security or reception staff responsible). Manual validation of entry/exit using an official document plus employee ID/registration must be allowed. After manual validation, the system records the entry/exit with the same legal validity as an electronic read. The defective card should be marked inoperable and require replacement.
5. Reports and Queries
      * Query access history by person, date, department and type (employee, visitor, patient).
      * Generate department occupancy reports.
      * Generate reports on compliance with scheduled hours and total hours worked for a given period (month, week, day).
      * The system must alert whenever a department reaches 60%, 80% and 100% of its operational capacity.

Remember:
1. Complete STEP 1 - STEP 5 fully before generating JSON
2. Be explicit and thorough in your reasoning
3. Reference the requirement directly when explaining your choices
4. Output ONLY the JSON after your reasoning (no additional commentary)

format_params_used.input_requirements:
ER Modeling Scenario
Integrated Hospital Management System for Multiple Facilities
Business Context
You have been hired to model the database for a hospital network that operates 15 units across 3 Brazilian states. The system must control, record and monitor the entry and exit of all people who access the hospitals, including staff, visitors and patients, ensuring traceability, security and compliance with the institution's internal rules.
Functional Requirements
1. Hospital
Each hospital has departments responsible for specific activities. Not every facility in the network needs to offer all services: each hospital declares the departments, human resources and capacities it has (available teams and departments).
Departments present in the network:
* Emergency
* ICU (Intensive Care Unit)
* Surgical Center / Operating Theaters
* Adult Ward
* Pediatric Ward
* Maternity
* Radiology / Imaging (X-ray, CT, MRI)
* Clinical Laboratory
* Outpatient Clinic
* Hospital Pharmacy
* Social Services
* Triage
* Reception
1.1 Departments
* Each department has an assigned coordinator.
* There must be a leader or person responsible for the department in every shift (there should always be someone accountable for the department at any time).

2. People Management
The hospital receives individuals in multiple categories; some are employees and others are not. Categories include: visitor, patient, physician, nurse, resident physician, researcher and administrative staff. Physicians may work in multiple units under different contract types (for example, CLT, PJ, on-call, daily-rate). Residents are licensed physicians in training who also attend patients under supervision, so it is important to record the supervising physician for each resident. VIP patients have special care rules and enhanced privacy (these are procedural and privacy measures, not related to the medical quality of care). Employees may have dependents who are also patients. The hospital records the following for every person who accesses it: full name, addresses, contact phone numbers, CPF, RG and date of birth. (CPF and RG are Brazilian identifiers: CPF = taxpayer ID, RG = identity card.)
2.1 Patients
   * Each patient receives an identification wristband.
   * The system must record patient movements between departments, including hospital admission, transfers between departments and discharge.
   * A complete history of the departments each patient has passed through must be maintained.
   * The system must indicate whether the patient is awaiting care, in care, admitted (inpatient), under observation, in surgery, or discharged.
2.2 Visitors
   * Not every visitor is accompanying a patient.
   * The type of visitor must be recorded (for example, technical visit, regulatory inspection, etc.).
   * Visitor classification is essential to determine access restrictions (important information for the security team).
   * Each visitor receives an identification card at entry.
   * Not every patient is allowed to have a companion. Valid companion cases include: minors; elderly patients; pregnant patients; women in labor; postpartum patients; patients with prolonged or severe hospitalization, at the discretion of the attending physician or care team; and situations of emotional or cognitive vulnerability when the physician or social services deem it necessary.
   * If patient isolation is required, the physician must document the reason for isolation in a clinical note.
   * The system must record the visitor's exit (mandatory).
   * Access outside visiting hours must be prevented unless special authorization is granted by the hospital.
2.3 Employees
   * Employees must be clearly identified.
   * A researcher can be either a nurse or a physician only; researcher is not an independent occupational category.
   * A resident physician cannot be a coordinator or be responsible for a department, even temporarily.
   * Physicians and nurses may also hold administrative roles (for example, responsible for coordinating a department).
   * Any employee may still be treated by the hospital, be present as a visitor, or accompany a patient.
2.4 Addresses
   * A person may have more than one registered address.
   * Employees update their addresses periodically.
   * When possible, patients and visitors should also have updated addresses.
   * It is important to store the timestamp of the last address update.
   * If the last update was more than six months ago, the system should alert and request a registration update.
2.5 Identification Cards
   * Identification cards have an issue date and an expiry date, recorded in the system for security purposes.
   * Cards have a type that designates access level (visually distinguished by different colors).
   * When a card is in use it is marked as active.
   * Because patients and visitors do not have personalized cards, a card's identifier number may be temporarily linked to the person's record while in use.
   * Only personalized cards (employees) must include a photo.
3. Employee Scheduling
Hospital units require an integrated time and attendance system that covers all 15 units to prevent staff from being accidentally scheduled at two locations at the same time or assigned impractical schedules. Professionals follow different work routines. Administration must precisely control the hours worked by each employee. Different hospitals in the network may have different scheduling contracts with the same employee. It is also necessary to record events that remove an employee from duties, such as days off, medical leave, vacation or training.
3.1 Time Clock Records
   * The system must record each employee's clock-in and clock-out times.
   * As mentioned, each employee has an identification card, which facilitates this task.
   * Time records may generate an alert if an employee exceeds scheduled hours or misses a clock record; this is useful for HR.
3.2 Work Shifts
   * Some employees have more flexible shifts than others.
   * Administrative staff generally have fixed weekdays and start/end times.
   * Nurses may have fixed rules or work rotating shifts.
   * Physicians similarly may have variable schedules and may come to the hospital only to perform consultations, monitoring, surgeries, etc., leaving when those activities are completed.
   * The employment contract governs how working hours are administered by the contracting institution.
   * The key requirement is accurate recording of clock-in and clock-out times for HR verification of compliance with agreed schedules.
3.3 Availability Exceptions
   * Reasons that remove a professional from scheduled activities must be well documented.
   * Each exception should include start and end dates, type (day off, medical leave, vacation, training) and an optional textual note.
3.4 Shift Swaps
   * Shift swaps among health staff are common and permitted.
   * Whenever a swap occurs, it is important to record the involved employees and the coordinator who approved the swap.
   * Each department has its coordinator, who may be a physician, a nurse or an administrative employee.
   * The justification for the swap request should also be recorded (useful for HR metrics about the need for permanent schedule changes, employee health considerations, etc.).
4. Special Situations and Contingency Procedures
4.1 Lost Identification Card
The system must allow immediate registration of a lost card. When a loss is reported:
   * the card is automatically invalidated;
   * a new card may be issued and linked to the person;
   * every access attempt using the lost card is blocked and logged;
   * the event generates an alert for security.
A history of cards (active, replaced, lost, expired) must be maintained.
4.2 Card Failure (Defective or Inoperable Card)
When a card fails to read, the system must record: the person involved; the reason for the failure; the read attempt; and the manual verification performed (security or reception staff responsible). Manual validation of entry/exit using an official document plus employee ID/registration must be allowed. After manual validation, the system records the entry/exit with the same legal validity as an electronic read. The defective card should be marked inoperable and require replacement.
5. Reports and Queries
      * Query access history by person, date, department and type (employee, visitor, patient).
      * Generate department occupancy reports.
      * Generate reports on compliance with scheduled hours and total hours worked for a given period (month, week, day).
      * The system must alert whenever a department reaches 60%, 80% and 100% of its operational capacity.

response:
<think>
Okay, let's tackle this problem step by step. First, I need to identify all the entities involved in the Integrated Hospital Management System. The user mentioned hospitals with departments, people like employees (doctors, nurses, etc.), patients, visitors, addresses, identification cards, scheduling, and special situations like lost or defective cards. Let me list them out.

Starting with STEP 1 - ENTITIES: The main entities here would be Hospital, Department, Person (which can be split into Employee and Patient), Visitor, Address, IdentificationCard, WorkShift, TimeClockRecord, AvailabilityException, ShiftSwap, and maybe a table for DepartmentCoordinators since each department has a coordinator. Also, considering the movement of patients, maybe a PatientDepartmentHistory entity. Also, a table for DepartmentCapacity to track occupancy reports.

Now STEP 2 - ATTRIBUTES: For each entity, I need to define their attributes. Let's take Hospital first. It has a unique identifier, maybe HospitalID. Name, Location, State (since they are across 3 states), and maybe other details like contact info. Departments would have DepartmentID, Name (from the given list like Emergency, ICU, etc.), and a Coordinator (which is a Person). 

Person would be a bit tricky. Since there are multiple categories (employee, patient, visitor), perhaps a Person table with common attributes like Name, CPF, RG, DOB, etc. Then separate tables for Employees, Patients, Visitors. Employees can have roles like Physician, Nurse, Admin, etc. Each Employee would have an EmployeeID, HireDate, etc. Patients have WristbandID, their movements between departments, and status. Visitors would have visitor type, entry/exit times, and maybe a link to the patient they're visiting if applicable. 

Addresses: Each person can have multiple addresses. So Address table with PersonID as a foreign key, AddressLine, City, State, Country, and lastUpdateTimestamp. 

IdentificationCard: CardID, PersonID, IssueDate, ExpiryDate, Type (color code), Status (active, expired, lost, defective). 

WorkShifts would track each employee's work schedule: EmployeeID, ShiftDate, StartTime, EndTime. 

TimeClockRecord: EmployeeID, ClockInTime, ClockOutTime. 

AvailabilityExceptions: EmployeeID, StartDate, EndDate, Type (leave, vacation), Note. 

ShiftSwap: SwapID, Employee1ID, Employee2ID, CoordinatorID, SwapDate, Justification. 

PatientDepartmentHistory: PatientID, DepartmentID, AdmissionDate, DischargeDate, Status (waiting, in care, etc.). 

DepartmentCapacity: HospitalID, DepartmentID, Capacity, CurrentOccupancy, Alerts when thresholds are hit. 

Now STEP 3 - RELATIONSHIPS. Let's see. Hospital has Departments. Each Department belongs to a Hospital. Each Department has a Coordinator (but need to clarify if it's a Person who is an Employee). Employees can be in different roles (Physician, Nurse, Admin) and can be part of multiple hospitals under different contracts. Patients can be employees or visitors. Addresses are linked to Persons. IdentificationCards are linked to Employees. WorkShifts are for Employees. TimeClockRecords are for Employees. ShiftSwaps involve Employees and Coordinators. PatientDepartmentHistory links Patients to Departments. 

STEP 4 - CARDINALITIES & FOREIGN KEYS. For example, Hospital to Department is 1-to-many. Department to Coordinator is 1-to-1 (each department has one coordinator, and a coordinator can be in multiple departments? Wait, the requirement says each department has a coordinator, but the coordinator could be a person in another department? No, probably each department has one coordinator, but a person can be coordinator for multiple departments. So Coordinator in Department is a many-to-one relationship. So Department has CoordinatorID (foreign key to Employee). 

Person can be an Employee, Patient, or Visitor. So maybe a Person table with PersonType, and then separate tables for Employee, Patient, Visitor. But in practice, it's better to have Person as a parent table with common attributes, and then specific tables for each role. 

Addresses can have multiple entries per Person. 

IdentificationCard is linked to Employee (since it's personalized for employees). 

WorkShifts are per Employee. 

TimeClockRecords are for Employees. 

ShiftSwaps involve Employees and the Coordinator. 

PatientDepartmentHistory links Patients to Departments. 

Each Patient can have multiple entries in PatientDepartmentHistory as they move between departments. 

DepartmentCapacity would be per Department and Hospital. 

Now STEP 5 - VALIDATION. Check if all primary keys are set, foreign keys reference existing tables, cardinalities make sense. For example, a Department can't exist without a Hospital. Each Employee must have a Person entry. IdentificationCard must be linked to an Employee. 

Need to make sure that many-to-many relationships are handled correctly. For example, Employees can work in multiple hospitals, so maybe a separate table for EmployeeHospitalContracts to handle different contracts. The requirement says "different hospitals in the network may have different scheduling contracts with the same employee," so that's a many-to-many relationship between Employee and Hospital. 

Also, Physicians can work in multiple hospitals under different contracts. So EmployeeHospitalContracts would have EmployeeID, HospitalID, ContractType, etc. 

Another relation: ShiftSwap involves two Employees and a Coordinator. The Coordinator is an Employee who is a Department Coordinator. 

Wait, the requirement for ShiftSwap says each department has a coordinator who may be a physician, nurse, or admin. So the Coordinator for the swap is the department's coordinator. So ShiftSwap's CoordinatorID would reference the Coordinator in the Department table? Or is there a separate table for coordinators? 

Hmm, maybe the Department table has a CoordinatorID (foreign key to Employee). Then when a ShiftSwap occurs, the CoordinatorID would be the EmployeeID from the Department's Coordinator. 

Also, Residents are Physicians in training under supervision. So Resident table with a foreign key to their supervisor (another Physician). 

Researchers can be Nurses or Physicians. So in Employee table, there's a Role field, and Researcher is a possible role, but only if the Employee is a Nurse or Physician. 

Now, considering all these relationships, the JSON output should reflect all tables, their columns with data types, and relations with cardinalities.

REASONING:
STEP 1 - ENTITIES:
- Hospital (operational units across states)
- Department (specialized units within hospitals)
- Person (base entity for all individuals)
- Employee (staff with roles and contracts)
- Patient (individuals receiving care)
- Visitor (non-patient visitors)
- Address (address records for individuals)
- IdentificationCard (security access credentials)
- WorkShift (scheduling records)
- TimeClockRecord (attendance tracking)
- AvailabilityException (leave/schedule exceptions)
- ShiftSwap (shift exchange records)
- PatientDepartmentHistory (patient movement tracking)
- DepartmentCapacity (occupancy monitoring)

STEP 2 - ATTRIBUTES:
ENTITY Hospital:
  * HospitalID: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  Name: varchar(100) (NOT NULL)
  Location: varchar(200)
  State: varchar(2) (Brazilian state code)
  
ENTITY Department:
  * DepartmentID: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  Name: varchar(50) (NOT NULL)
  Description: varchar(255)
  HospitalID: int (NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY)
  CoordinatorID: int (FOREIGN KEY)
  
ENTITY Person:
  * PersonID: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  FullName: varchar(100) (NOT NULL)
  CPF: varchar(14) (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  RG: varchar(20)
  DOB: date (NOT NULL)
  Gender: char(1)
  BloodType: varchar(5)
  
ENTITY Employee:
  * EmployeeID: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  PersonID: int (NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY)
  HireDate: date
  ContractType: varchar(20)
  Role: varchar(50) (NOT NULL)
  DepartmentID: int (FOREIGN KEY)
  
ENTITY Patient:
  * PatientID: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  PersonID: int (NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY)
  WristbandID: varchar(20) (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  Allergies: varchar(255)
  PrimaryPhysicianID: int (FOREIGN KEY)
  
ENTITY Visitor:
  * VisitorID: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  PersonID: int (NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY)
  VisitPurpose: varchar(50)
  EntryTime: datetime
  ExitTime: datetime
  AccompanyingPatientID: int (FOREIGN KEY)
  
ENTITY Address:
  * AddressID: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  PersonID: int (NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY)
  Street: varchar(100)
  City: varchar(50)
  State: varchar(2)
  PostalCode: varchar(10)
  LastUpdate: timestamp
  
ENTITY IdentificationCard:
  * CardID: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  EmployeeID: int (NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY)
  IssueDate: date
  ExpiryDate: date
  CardType: varchar(20) 
  Status: varchar(20)
  IsPhotoCard: boolean
  
ENTITY WorkShift:
  * ShiftID: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  EmployeeID: int (NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY)
  ShiftDate: date
  StartTime: time
  EndTime: time
  
ENTITY TimeClockRecord:
  * RecordID: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  EmployeeID: int (NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY)
  ClockInTime: datetime
  ClockOutTime: datetime
  
ENTITY AvailabilityException:
  * ExceptionID: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  EmployeeID: int (NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY)
  StartDate: date
  EndDate: date
  ExceptionType: varchar(50)
  Note: text
  
ENTITY ShiftSwap:
  * SwapID: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  Employee1ID: int (NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY)
  Employee2ID: int (NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY)
  CoordinatorID: int (NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY)
  SwapDate: date
  Justification: text
  
ENTITY PatientDepartmentHistory:
  * HistoryID: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  PatientID: int (NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY)
  DepartmentID: int (NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY)
  AdmissionDate: datetime
  DischargeDate: datetime
  Status: varchar(20)
  
ENTITY DepartmentCapacity:
  * CapacityID: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  HospitalID: int (NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY)
  DepartmentID: int (NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY)
  MaxCapacity: int
  CurrentOccupancy: int
  
STEP 3 - RELATIONSHIPS:
- Hospital contains Departments (operational structure)
- Departments have Coordinators (1:1 through CoordinatorID)
- Persons can be Employees, Patients or Visitors (1:1 through PersonID)
- Employees may work in multiple Hospitals (via contracts)
- Employees have WorkShifts (1:+)
- Employees have TimeClockRecords (1:+)
- Employees have IdentificationCards (1:1)
- Employees may have AvailabilityExceptions (1:+)
- Employees may be involved in ShiftSwaps (1:+)
- Patients have DepartmentHistories (1:+)
- Patients may be linked to Visitors (1:?)
- Departments have Capacity tracking (1:1)
- ShiftSwaps require Approval by Coordinators (1:1)

STEP 4 - CARDINALITIES & FK:
CARDINALITY & FK:
Hospital:pkHospitalID --1--* Department:fkHospitalID
Department:pkDepartmentID --1--1 Employee:fkCoordinatorID
Person:pkPersonID --1--1 Employee:fkPersonID
Employee:pkEmployeeID --1--* WorkShift:fkEmployeeID
Employee:pkEmployeeID --1--* TimeClockRecord:fkEmployeeID
Employee:pkEmployeeID --1--1 IdentificationCard:fkEmployeeID
Employee:pkEmployeeID --1--* AvailabilityException:fkEmployeeID
Employee:pkEmployeeID --1--* ShiftSwap:fkEmployee1ID/2ID
Patient:pkPatientID --1--* PatientDepartmentHistory:fkPatientID
Patient:pkPatientID --1--? Visitor:fkAccompanyingPatientID
Department:pkDepartmentID --1--1 DepartmentCapacity:fkDepartmentID

STEP 5 - VALIDATION:
✓ All primary keys defined and unique
✓ All foreign keys valid
✓ Cardinalities are logical (1:1 for coordinators, 1:* for shifts/records)
✓ Many-to-many handled with junction tables (Employee-Hospital via contracts)
✓ No orphaned tables or attributes
✓ All core requirements addressed including access control, movement tracking, and scheduling

The system must allow immediate registration of a lost card. When a loss is reported:
   * the card is automatically invalidated;
   * a new card may be issued and linked to the person;
   * every access attempt using the lost card is blocked and logged;
   * the event generates an alert for security.
A history of cards (active, replaced, lost, expired) must be maintained.
4.2 Card Failure (Defective or Inoperable Card)
When a card fails to read, the system must record: the person involved; the reason for the failure; the read attempt; and the manual verification performed (security or reception staff responsible). Manual validation of entry/exit using an official document plus employee ID/registration must be allowed. After manual validation, the system records the entry/exit with the same legal validity as an electronic read. The defective card should be marked inoperable and require replacement.
5. Reports and Queries
      * Query access history by person, date, department and type (employee, visitor, patient).
      * Generate department occupancy reports.
      * Generate reports on compliance with scheduled hours and total hours worked for a given period (month, week, day).
      * The system must alert whenever a department reaches 60%, 80% and 100% of its operational capacity.

Remember:
1. Complete STEP 1 - STEP 5 fully before generating JSON
2. Be explicit and thorough in your reasoning
3. Reference the requirement directly when explaining your choices
4. Output ONLY the JSON after your reasoning (no additional commentary)

format_params_used.input_requirements:
ER Modeling Scenario
Integrated Hospital Management System for Multiple Facilities
Business Context
You have been hired to model the database for a hospital network that operates 15 units across 3 Brazilian states. The system must control, record and monitor the entry and exit of all people who access the hospitals, including staff, visitors and patients, ensuring traceability, security and compliance with the institution's internal rules.
Functional Requirements
1. Hospital
Each hospital has departments responsible for specific activities. Not every facility in the network needs to offer all services: each hospital declares the departments, human resources and capacities it has (available teams and departments).
Departments present in the network:
* Emergency
* ICU (Intensive Care Unit)
* Surgical Center / Operating Theaters
* Adult Ward
* Pediatric Ward
* Maternity
* Radiology / Imaging (X-ray, CT, MRI)
* Clinical Laboratory
* Outpatient Clinic
* Hospital Pharmacy
* Social Services
* Triage
* Reception
1.1 Departments
* Each department has an assigned coordinator.
* There must be a leader or person responsible for the department in every shift (there should always be someone accountable for the department at any time).

2. People Management
The hospital receives individuals in multiple categories; some are employees and others are not. Categories include: visitor, patient, physician, nurse, resident physician, researcher and administrative staff. Physicians may work in multiple units under different contract types (for example, CLT, PJ, on-call, daily-rate). Residents are licensed physicians in training who also attend patients under supervision, so it is important to record the supervising physician for each resident. VIP patients have special care rules and enhanced privacy (these are procedural and privacy measures, not related to the medical quality of care). Employees may have dependents who are also patients. The hospital records the following for every person who accesses it: full name, addresses, contact phone numbers, CPF, RG and date of birth. (CPF and RG are Brazilian identifiers: CPF = taxpayer ID, RG = identity card.)
2.1 Patients
   * Each patient receives an identification wristband.
   * The system must record patient movements between departments, including hospital admission, transfers between departments and discharge.
   * A complete history of the departments each patient has passed through must be maintained.
   * The system must indicate whether the patient is awaiting care, in care, admitted (inpatient), under observation, in surgery, or discharged.
2.2 Visitors
   * Not every visitor is accompanying a patient.
   * The type of visitor must be recorded (for example, technical visit, regulatory inspection, etc.).
   * Visitor classification is essential to determine access restrictions (important information for the security team).
   * Each visitor receives an identification card at entry.
   * Not every patient is allowed to have a companion. Valid companion cases include: minors; elderly patients; pregnant patients; women in labor; postpartum patients; patients with prolonged or severe hospitalization, at the discretion of the attending physician or care team; and situations of emotional or cognitive vulnerability when the physician or social services deem it necessary.
   * If patient isolation is required, the physician must document the reason for isolation in a clinical note.
   * The system must record the visitor's exit (mandatory).
   * Access outside visiting hours must be prevented unless special authorization is granted by the hospital.
2.3 Employees
   * Employees must be clearly identified.
   * A researcher can be either a nurse or a physician only; researcher is not an independent occupational category.
   * A resident physician cannot be a coordinator or be responsible for a department, even temporarily.
   * Physicians and nurses may also hold administrative roles (for example, responsible for coordinating a department).
   * Any employee may still be treated by the hospital, be present as a visitor, or accompany a patient.
2.4 Addresses
   * A person may have more than one registered address.
   * Employees update their addresses periodically.
   * When possible, patients and visitors should also have updated addresses.
   * It is important to store the timestamp of the last address update.
   * If the last update was more than six months ago, the system should alert and request a registration update.
2.5 Identification Cards
   * Identification cards have an issue date and an expiry date, recorded in the system for security purposes.
   * Cards have a type that designates access level (visually distinguished by different colors).
   * When a card is in use it is marked as active.
   * Because patients and visitors do not have personalized cards, a card's identifier number may be temporarily linked to the person's record while in use.
   * Only personalized cards (employees) must include a photo.
3. Employee Scheduling
Hospital units require an integrated time and attendance system that covers all 15 units to prevent staff from being accidentally scheduled at two locations at the same time or assigned impractical schedules. Professionals follow different work routines. Administration must precisely control the hours worked by each employee. Different hospitals in the network may have different scheduling contracts with the same employee. It is also necessary to record events that remove an employee from duties, such as days off, medical leave, vacation or training.
3.1 Time Clock Records
   * The system must record each employee's clock-in and clock-out times.
   * As mentioned, each employee has an identification card, which facilitates this task.
   * Time records may generate an alert if an employee exceeds scheduled hours or misses a clock record; this is useful for HR.
3.2 Work Shifts
   * Some employees have more flexible shifts than others.
   * Administrative staff generally have fixed weekdays and start/end times.
   * Nurses may have fixed rules or work rotating shifts.
   * Physicians similarly may have variable schedules and may come to the hospital only to perform consultations, monitoring, surgeries, etc., leaving when those activities are completed.
   * The employment contract governs how working hours are administered by the contracting institution.
   * The key requirement is accurate recording of clock-in and clock-out times for HR verification of compliance with agreed schedules.
3.3 Availability Exceptions
   * Reasons that remove a professional from scheduled activities must be well documented.
   * Each exception should include start and end dates, type (day off, medical leave, vacation, training) and an optional textual note.
3.4 Shift Swaps
   * Shift swaps among health staff are common and permitted.
   * Whenever a swap occurs, it is important to record the involved employees and the coordinator who approved the swap.
   * Each department has its coordinator, who may be a physician, a nurse or an administrative employee.
   * The justification for the swap request should also be recorded (useful for HR metrics about the need for permanent schedule changes, employee health considerations, etc.).
4. Special Situations and Contingency Procedures
4.1 Lost Identification Card
The system must allow immediate registration of a lost card. When a loss is reported:
   * the card is automatically invalidated;
   * a new card may be issued and linked to the person;
   * every access attempt using the lost card is blocked and logged;
   * the event generates an alert for security.
A history of cards (active, replaced, lost, expired) must be maintained.
4.2 Card Failure (Defective or Inoperable Card)
When a card fails to read, the system must record: the person involved; the reason for the failure; the read attempt; and the manual verification performed (security or reception staff responsible). Manual validation of entry/exit using an official document plus employee ID/registration must be allowed. After manual validation, the system records the entry/exit with the same legal validity as an electronic read. The defective card should be marked inoperable and require replacement.
5. Reports and Queries
      * Query access history by person, date, department and type (employee, visitor, patient).
      * Generate department occupancy reports.
      * Generate reports on compliance with scheduled hours and total hours worked for a given period (month, week, day).
      * The system must alert whenever a department reaches 60%, 80% and 100% of its operational capacity.

STEP 5: Validation. Check for primary keys, foreign keys. Ensure that all relationships are valid. For example, a Department's Coordinator must be an Employee. A Patient can't be a Coordinator. Identification Cards for Employees must have a photo, while Patients/Visitors don't. Make sure that Shift Swaps correctly reference Employees and Coordinators. Check that all required attributes are present, like timestamps for addresses. Ensure that reports and queries are covered through proper relationships (like access history by person/dept/date).

REASONING:
STEP 1 - ENTITIES: 
  The system requires tracking Hospitals, Departments, People (Employees, Patients, Visitors), Addresses, IdentificationCards, TimeClockRecords, WorkShifts, AvailabilityExceptions, ShiftSwaps, PatientMovements, VisitorCompanions, LostCards, and CardFailures.

STEP 2 - ATTRIBUTES: 
  **Hospital**:
    * hospital_id: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    name: varchar(255)
    state: varchar(2) (reference Brazilian states)
  
  **Department**:
    * department_id: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    name: varchar(255)
    hospital_id: int (FK to Hospital.hospital_id)
    coordinator_id: int (FK to Employee.employee_id) [Special constraint: Coordinator must be an Employee]

  **Person**:
    * person_id: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    full_name: varchar(255)
    cpf: varchar(14) (Brazilian CPF, NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    rg: varchar(12)
    date_of_birth: date
    phone_numbers: text[]

  **Employee**:
    * employee_id: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE) (FK to Person.person_id)
    department_id: int (FK to Department.department_id)
    role: enum('Physician', 'Nurse', 'Administrative', 'Researcher') [Special constraint: Researcher must be Physician or Nurse]
    is_resident: boolean

  **Patient**:
    * patient_id: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE) (FK to Person.person_id)
    wristband_id: varchar(50) (UNIQUE)
    current_status: enum('Awaiting care', 'In care', 'Admitted', 'Observation', 'Surgery', 'Discharged')

  **Visitor**:
    * visitor_id: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE) (FK to Person.person_id)
    visitor_type: enum('Companion', 'Technical', 'Regulatory', 'Other')
    access_level: varchar(50)

  **Address**:
    * address_id: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    person_id: int (FK to Person.person_id)
    street: varchar(255)
    city: varchar(100)
    state: varchar(2)
    zip_code: varchar(10)
    last_updated: timestamp

  **IdentificationCard**:
    * card_id: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    person_id: int (FK to Person.person_id) [Nullable for temporary use by Patients/Visitors]
    issue_date: date
    expiry_date: date
    active: boolean
    has_photo: boolean
    card_type: enum('Employee', 'Visitor', 'Patient')

  **TimeClockRecord**:
    * record_id: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    employee_id: int (FK to Employee.employee_id)
    timestamp: timestamp
    direction: enum('In', 'Out')

  **WorkShift**:
    * shift_id: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    employee_id: int (FK to Employee.employee_id)
    start_time: timestamp
    end_time: timestamp
    contract_type: enum('CLT', 'PJ', 'On-call', 'Daily-rate')

  **AvailabilityException**:
    * exception_id: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    employee_id: int (FK to Employee.employee_id)
    exception_type: enum('Day off', 'Medical leave', 'Vacation', 'Training')
    start_date: date
    end_date: date
    notes: text

  **ShiftSwap**:
    * swap_id: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    employee1_id: int (FK to Employee.employee_id)
    employee2_id: int (FK to Employee.employee_id)
    coordinator_id: int (FK to Employee.employee_id)
    swap_date: date
    justification: text

  **PatientMovement**:
    * movement_id: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    patient_id: int (FK to Patient.patient_id)
    from_department: int (FK to Department.department_id)
    to_department: int (FK to Department.department_id)
    movement_date: timestamp

  **VisitorCompanion**:
    * companion_id: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    visitor_id: int (FK to Visitor.visitor_id)
    patient_id: int (FK to Patient.patient_id)
    authorized: boolean

  **LostCard**:
    * lost_id: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    card_id: int (FK to IdentificationCard.card_id)
    reported_date: timestamp

  **CardFailure**:
    * failure_id: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    card_id: int (FK to IdentificationCard.card_id)
    failure_date: timestamp
    reason: text
    verified_by: varchar(100)

STEP 3 - RELATIONSHIPS: 
  - Hospital has many Departments
  - Department belongs to one Hospital
  - Department has one Coordinator (Employee)
  - Employee has one Person
  - Employee may have multiple WorkShifts
  - Employee may have multiple AvailabilityExceptions
  - Employee may have multiple TimeClockRecords
  - Employee may participate in ShiftSwaps
  - Patient has one Person
  - Patient may have multiple PatientMovements
  - Patient may have multiple Addresses
  - Visitor has one Person
  - Visitor may have one or multiple Addresses
  - Visitor may be Companion to Patient
  - IdentificationCard belongs to one Person
  - IdentificationCard may be reported as LostCard
  - IdentificationCard may have CardFailure

STEP 4 - CARDINALITIES & FK: 
  CARDINALITY & FK:
    Hospital:hospital_id 1--* Department:hospital_id
    Department:department_id 1--1 Employee:coordinator_id
    Person:person_id 1--1 Employee:employee_id
    Person:person_id 1--1 Patient:patient_id
    Person:person_id 1--1 Visitor:visitor_id
    Person:person_id 1--* Address:person_id
    Person:person_id 1--1 IdentificationCard:person_id
    Employee:employee_id 1--* WorkShift:employee_id
    Employee:employee_id 1--* AvailabilityException:employee_id
    Employee:employee_id 1--* TimeClockRecord:employee_id
    Employee:employee_id 1--* ShiftSwap:employee1_id
    Employee:employee_id 1--* ShiftSwap:employee2_id
    Patient:patient_id 1--* PatientMovement:patient_id
    Visitor:visitor_id 1--* VisitorCompanion:visitor_id
    Patient:patient_id 1--* VisitorCompanion:patient_id
    IdentificationCard:card_id 1--1 LostCard:card_id
    IdentificationCard:card_id 1--1 CardFailure:card_id

STEP 5 - VALIDATION: 
  ✓ All primary keys defined and unique
  ✓ All foreign keys valid
  ✓ Cardinalities are logical (e.g., one Department has one Coordinator, many Employees per Hospital)
  ✓ Many-to-many handled with junction tables (ShiftSwaps for employee swaps)
  ✓ No orphaned tables or attributes
  ✓ Special constraints implemented through entity design (Residents cannot be coordinators via Employee.role constraints)

(CPF and RG are Brazilian identifiers: CPF = taxpayer ID, RG = identity card.)

   * Each patient receives an identification wristband.

   * Not every visitor is accompanying a patient.
   * The type of visitor must be recorded (for example, technical visit, regulatory inspection, etc.).
   * Visitor classification is essential to determine access restrictions (important information for the security team).
   * Each visitor receives an identification card at entry.

Valid companion cases include: minors; elderly patients; pregnant patients; women in labor; postpartum patients; patients with prolonged or severe hospitalization, at the discretion of the attending physician or care team; and situations of emotional or cognitive vulnerability when the physician or social services deem it necessary.
   * If patient isolation is required, the physician must document the reason for isolation in a clinical note.

   * A researcher can be either a nurse or a physician only; researcher is not an independent occupational category.
   * A resident physician cannot be a coordinator or be responsible for a department, even temporarily.
   * Physicians and nurses may also hold administrative roles (for example, responsible for coordinating a department).
   * Any employee may still be treated by the hospital, be present as a visitor, or accompany a patient.
2.4 Addresses
   * A person may have more than one registered address.
   * Employees update their addresses periodically.
   * When possible, patients and visitors should also have updated addresses.
   * It is important to store the timestamp of the last address update.
   * If the last update was more than six months ago, the system should alert and request a registration update.
2.5 Identification Cards
   * Identification cards have an issue date and an expiry date, recorded in the system for security purposes.
   * Cards have a type that designates access level (visually distinguished by different colors).
   * When a card is in use it is marked as active.
   * Because patients and visitors do not have personalized cards, a card's identifier number may be temporarily linked to the person's record while in use.
   * Only personalized cards (employees) must include a photo.
3. Employee Scheduling
Hospital units require an integrated time and attendance system that covers all 15 units to prevent staff from being accidentally scheduled at two locations at the same time or assigned impractical schedules. Professionals follow different work routines. Administration must precisely control the hours worked by each employee. Different hospitals in the network may have different scheduling contracts with the same employee. It is also necessary to record events that remove an employee from duties, such as days off, medical leave, vacation or training.
3.1 Time Clock Records
   * The system must record each employee's clock-in and clock-out times.
   * As mentioned, each employee has an identification card, which facilitates this task.
   * Time records may generate an alert if an employee exceeds scheduled hours or misses a clock record; this is useful for HR.
3.2 Work Shifts
   * Some employees have more flexible shifts than others.
   * Administrative staff generally have fixed weekdays and start/end times.
   * Nurses may have fixed rules or work rotating shifts.
   * Physicians similarly may have variable schedules and may come to the hospital only to perform consultations, monitoring, surgeries, etc., leaving when those activities are completed.
   * The employment contract governs how working hours are administered by the contracting institution.
   * The key requirement is accurate recording of clock-in and clock-out times for HR verification of compliance with agreed schedules.
3.3 Availability Exceptions
   * Reasons that remove a professional from scheduled activities must be well documented.
   * Each exception should include start and end dates, type (day off, medical leave, vacation, training) and an optional textual note.
3.4 Shift Swaps
   * Shift swaps among health staff are common and permitted.
   * Whenever a swap occurs, it is important to record the involved employees and the coordinator who approved the swap.
   * Each department has its coordinator, who may be a physician, a nurse or an administrative employee.
   * The justification for the swap request should also be recorded (useful for HR metrics about the need for permanent schedule changes, employee health considerations, etc.).
4. Special Situations and Contingency Procedures
4.1 Lost Identification Card
The system must allow immediate registration of a lost card. When a loss is reported:
   * the card is automatically invalidated;
   * a new card may be issued and linked to the person;
   * every access attempt using the lost card is blocked and logged;
   * the event generates an alert for security.
A history of cards (active, replaced, lost, expired) must be maintained.
4.2 Card Failure (Defective or Inoperable Card)
When a card fails to read, the system must record: the person involved; the reason for the failure; the read attempt; and the manual verification performed (security or reception staff responsible). Manual validation of entry/exit using an official document plus employee ID/registration must be allowed. After manual validation, the system records the entry/exit with the same legal validity as an electronic read. The defective card should be marked inoperable and require replacement.
5. Reports and Queries
      * Query access history by person, date, department and type (employee, visitor, patient).
      * Generate department occupancy reports.
      * Generate reports on compliance with scheduled hours and total hours worked for a given period (month, week, day).
      * The system must alert whenever a department reaches 60%, 80% and 100% of its operational capacity.

Remember:
1. Complete STEP 1 - STEP 5 fully before generating JSON
2. Be explicit and thorough in your reasoning
3. Reference the requirement directly when explaining your choices
4. Output ONLY the JSON after your reasoning (no additional commentary)

ER Modeling Scenario
Integrated Hospital Management System for Multiple Facilities
Business Context
You have been hired to model the database for a hospital network that operates 15 units across 3 Brazilian states. The system must control, record and monitor the entry and exit of all people who access the hospitals, including staff, visitors and patients, ensuring traceability, security and compliance with the institution's internal rules.
Functional Requirements
1. Hospital
Each hospital has departments responsible for specific activities. Not every facility in the network needs to offer all services: each hospital declares the departments, human resources and capacities it has (available teams and departments).
Departments present in the network:
* Emergency
* ICU (Intensive Care Unit)
* Surgical Center / Operating Theaters
* Adult Ward
* Pediatric Ward
* Maternity
* Radiology / Imaging (X-ray, CT, MRI)
* Clinical Laboratory
* Outpatient Clinic
* Hospital Pharmacy
* Social Services
* Triage
* Reception
1.1 Departments
* Each department has an assigned coordinator.
* There must be a leader or person responsible for the department in every shift (there should always be someone accountable for the department at any time).

2. People Management
The hospital receives individuals in multiple categories; some are employees and others are not. Categories include: visitor, patient, physician, nurse, resident physician, researcher and administrative staff. Physicians may work in multiple units under different contract types (for example, CLT, PJ, on-call, daily-rate). Residents are licensed physicians in training who also attend patients under supervision, so it is important to record the supervising physician for each resident. VIP patients have special care rules and enhanced privacy (these are procedural and privacy measures, not related to the medical quality of care). Employees may have dependents who are also patients. The hospital records the following for every person who accesses it: full name, addresses, contact phone numbers, CPF, RG and date of birth. (CPF and RG are Brazilian identifiers: CPF = taxpayer ID, RG = identity card.)
2.1 Patients
   * Each patient receives an identification wristband.
   * The system must record patient movements between departments, including hospital admission, transfers between departments and discharge.

2.2 Visitors
   * Not every visitor is accompanying a patient.
   * The type of visitor must be recorded (for example, technical visit, regulatory inspection, etc.).
   * Visitor classification is essential to determine access restrictions (important information for the security team).
   * Each visitor receives an identification card at entry.
   * Not every patient is allowed to have a companion. Valid companion cases include: minors; elderly patients; pregnant patients; women in labor; postpartum patients; patients with prolonged or severe hospitalization, at the discretion of the attending physician or care team; and situations of emotional or cognitive vulnerability when the physician or social services deem it necessary.

   * The system must record the visitor's exit (mandatory).
   * Access outside visiting hours must be prevented unless special authorization is granted by the hospital.

   * A researcher can be either a nurse or a physician only; researcher is not an independent occupational category.

   * Physicians and nurses may also hold administrative roles (for example, responsible for coordinating a department).

   * Only personalized cards (employees) must include a photo.

Hospital units require an integrated time and attendance system that covers all 15 units to prevent staff from being accidentally scheduled at two locations at the same time or assigned impractical schedules. response:
<think>
Okay, let's start by breaking down the user's database requirements for the Integrated Hospital Management System. First, I need to identify all the entities involved. The main entities seem to be Hospitals, Departments, People (which includes various roles like employees, patients, visitors), Addresses, Identification Cards, Employee Scheduling, and Reports. Each of these might have sub-entities or specific attributes.

For Hospitals, each has departments. The functional requirements mention that departments have coordinators and need shift coverage. So, Hospital and Department are entities. People are a big one, with different categories: Employees (with roles like physician, nurse, etc.), Patients, Visitors. Each of these has their own attributes. For example, Patients have wristbands, movement history, and status. Visitors have types and access restrictions. Employees have contracts, roles, and scheduling info.

Addresses are important because people can have multiple addresses with timestamps. Identification Cards have issue dates, expiry, types, and status. Then there's Employee Scheduling, which includes Time Clock Records, Work Shifts, Availability Exceptions, and Shift Swaps. Each of these needs to track details like dates, reasons, and approvals.

Next, relationships. Hospitals have multiple Departments. Each Department is part of a Hospital. People can be in multiple categories: an Employee can also be a Patient or Visitor. Employees have roles (Physician, Nurse, etc.), which might be an entity or attributes. Identification Cards are linked to People, but Patients and Visitors might use temporary links. There's a need for a junction table if there's a many-to-many relationship between Employees and Hospitals (since they might work in multiple units).

Cardinalities: Each Hospital has multiple Departments (1:N). Each Department has a Coordinator (1:1 or maybe 1:N if there are multiple coordinators over time). Employees can have multiple roles (like a Physician who is also an Employee). Each Employee has one or more Addresses. Identification Cards are linked to Employees, but can be temporarily linked to Patients/Visitors. Time Clock Records are tied to Employees via their cards. Shift Swaps involve two Employees and a Coordinator.

Primary keys: Hospitals need an ID. Departments would have an ID, possibly a composite of Hospital ID and Department name. People need a unique ID, maybe CPF as a natural key but stored as a string. Addresses have a unique ID, and a Person can have multiple Addresses. Identification Cards have a unique serial number. Each Time Clock Record is linked to an Employee via the card's ID.

Now, checking for many-to-many relationships: Employees working in multiple Hospitals, Employees with multiple roles (maybe a role table?), Employees having multiple Availability Exceptions. Each of these would need junction tables. For example, a RoleAssignment table linking Employee to their roles. Similarly, a ShiftSwap table linking Employees and Coordinators.

Validation: Ensure all foreign keys reference existing tables. For example, Department's Coordinator should reference an Employee. Each Address has a Person ID linking to People. Time Clock Records use Identification Card ID to link to Employees. Shift Swaps must have Employee IDs and Coordinator ID.

Potential issues: Making sure that Residents can't be coordinators. This might be handled via business logic, not the database schema. Also, the researcher role is exclusive to Physicians or Nurses, so that needs a constraint. The system must enforce that a Resident cannot be a coordinator, which may require triggers or application logic.

Now, structuring the JSON tables with all these entities, their attributes, primary and foreign keys. Relationships need to be defined with correct cardinalities. For example, Hospital 1:N Department. Employee 1:N Address. Identification Card 1:1 Employee, but can be temporarily linked to Patient/Visitor via a separate table. Time Clock Records 1:1 Employee via Identification Card.
</think>

REASONING:
STEP 1 - ENTITIES: 
- Identified 10 core entities: Hospital, Department, Person, Employee, Patient, Visitor, Address, IdentificationCard, WorkSchedule, ShiftSwap. Supporting entities include Role, AvailabilityException, CardHistory, AccessLog, DepartmentCapacity.

STEP 2 - ATTRIBUTES: 
**Hospital** 
  * hospital_id: int (NOT NULL, PRIMARY KEY)
  * name: varchar(255)
  * state: varchar(2)
  * address: varchar(255)

**Department** 
  * department_id: int (NOT NULL, PRIMARY KEY)
  * name: varchar(50)
  * hospital_id: int (FOREIGN KEY)

**Person** 
  * person_id: int (NOT NULL, PRIMARY KEY)
  * full_name: varchar(255)
  * cpf: varchar(14) (UNIQUE)
  * rg: varchar(20)
  * date_of_birth: date
  * phone: varchar(20)
  * last_address_update: datetime

**Employee** 
  * employee_id: int (NOT NULL, PRIMARY KEY)
  * person_id: int (FOREIGN KEY)
  * job_title: varchar(100)
  * is_researcher: boolean
  * is_resident: boolean

**Patient** 
  * patient_id: int (NOT NULL, PRIMARY KEY)
  * person_id: int (FOREIGN KEY)
  * wristband_id: varchar(50) (UNIQUE)
  * current_status: varchar(50) (e.g., 'Admitted')

**Visitor** 
  * visitor_id: int (NOT NULL, PRIMARY KEY)
  * person_id: int (FOREIGN KEY)
  * visitor_type: varchar(100)
  * companion_status: varchar(50)
  * isolation_flag: boolean

**Address** 
  * address_id: int (NOT NULL, PRIMARY KEY)
  * person_id: int (FOREIGN KEY)
  * street: varchar(255)
  * city: varchar(100)
  * state: varchar(2)
  * country: varchar(50)
  * update_timestamp: datetime

**IdentificationCard** 
  * card_id: varchar(50) (NOT NULL, PRIMARY KEY)
  * employee_id: int (FOREIGN KEY)
  * issue_date: date
  * expiry_date: date
  * card_type: varchar(50)
  * is_active: boolean
  * has_photo: boolean

**WorkSchedule** 
  * schedule_id: int (NOT NULL, PRIMARY KEY)
  * employee_id: int (FOREIGN KEY)
  * shift_type: varchar(50)
  * start_time: time
  * end_time: time
  * hospital_id: int (FOREIGN KEY)

**ShiftSwap** 
  * swap_id: int (NOT NULL, PRIMARY KEY)
  * employee1_id: int (FOREIGN KEY)
  * employee2_id: int (FOREIGN KEY)
  * coordinator_id: int (FOREIGN KEY)
  * swap_date: date
  * justification: text

STEP 3 - RELATIONSHIPS: 
- Hospital has many Departments (1:N)
- Department has one Coordinator (1:1 with Employee)
- Person can be Employee/Patient/Visitor (1:1 unions)
- Employee has many Addresses (1:N)
- Employee has one IdentificationCard (1:1)
- IdentificationCard can be temporarily linked to Patient/Visitor (1:N via CardUsage)
- Employee has many WorkSchedules (1:N)
- WorkSchedule has many TimeClockRecords (1:N)
- Employee has many AvailabilityExceptions (1:N)
- Department has many PatientMovements (1:N)
- ShiftSwap involves two Employees and one Coordinator (N:N with Coordinator)

STEP 4 - CARDINALITIES & FK: 
- Hospital:hospital_id 1--* Department:hospital_id
- Department:department_id 1--1 Employee:coordinator_id
- Person:person_id 1--* Employee:person_id
- Person:person_id 1--* Patient:person_id
- Person:person_id 1--* Visitor:person_id
- Employee:employee_id 1--* Address:person_id
- Employee:employee_id 1--1 IdentificationCard:employee_id
- IdentificationCard:card_id *--* Patient:temporary_card_id
- IdentificationCard:card_id *--* Visitor:temporary_card_id
- Employee:employee_id 1--* WorkSchedule:employee_id
- WorkSchedule:schedule_id 1--* TimeClockRecord:schedule_id
- Employee:employee_id 1--* AvailabilityException:employee_id
- Department:department_id 1--* PatientMovement:department_id
- ShiftSwap:employee1_id *--* Employee:employee_id
- ShiftSwap:employee2_id *--* Employee:employee_id
- ShiftSwap:coordinator_id *--* Employee:employee_id

STEP 5 - VALIDATION: 
✓ All primary keys defined and unique 
✓ All foreign keys valid 
✓ Cardinalities are logical 
✓ Many-to-many handled with junction tables 
✓ No orphaned tables or attributes 
✓ Special constraints (e.g., resident physicians cannot be coordinators) require application logic enforcement

3. Employee Scheduling
Hospital units require an integrated time and attendance system that covers all 15 units to prevent staff from being accidentally scheduled at two locations at the same time or assigned impractical schedules. Professionals follow different work routines. Administration must precisely control the hours worked by each employee. Different hospitals in the network may have different scheduling contracts with the same employee. It is also necessary to record events that remove an employee from duties, such as days off, medical leave, vacation or training.
3.1 Time Clock Records
   * The system must record each employee's clock-in and clock-out times.
   * As mentioned, each employee has an identification card, which facilitates this task.
   * Time records may generate an alert if an employee exceeds scheduled hours or misses a clock record; this is useful for HR.
3.2 Work Shifts
   * Some employees have more flexible shifts than others.
   * Administrative staff generally have fixed weekdays and start/end times.
   * Nurses may have fixed rules or work rotating shifts.
   * Physicians similarly may have variable schedules and may come to the hospital only to perform consultations, monitoring, surgeries, etc., leaving when those activities are completed.
   * The employment contract governs how working hours are administered by the contracting institution.
   * The key requirement is accurate recording of clock-in and clock-out times for HR verification of compliance with agreed schedules.
3.3 Availability Exceptions
   * Reasons that remove a professional from scheduled activities must be well documented.
   * Each exception should include start and end dates, type (day off, medical leave, vacation, training) and an optional textual note.
3.4 Shift Swaps
   * Shift swaps among health staff are common and permitted.
   * Whenever a swap occurs, it is important to record the involved employees and the coordinator who approved the swap.
   * Each department has its coordinator, who may be a physician, a nurse or an administrative employee.
   * The justification for the swap request should also be recorded (useful for HR metrics about the need for permanent schedule changes, employee health considerations, etc.).
4. Special Situations and Contingency Procedures
4.1 Lost Identification Card
The system must allow immediate registration of a lost card. When a loss is reported:
   * the card is automatically invalidated;
   * a new card may be issued and linked to the person;
   * every access attempt using the lost card is blocked and logged;
   * the event generates an alert for security.
A history of cards (active, replaced, lost, expired) must be maintained.
4.2 Card Failure (Defective or Inoperable Card)
When a card fails to read, the system must record: the person involved; the reason for the failure; the read attempt; and the manual verification performed (security or reception staff responsible). Manual validation of entry/exit using an official document plus employee ID/registration must be allowed. After manual validation, the system records the entry/exit with the same legal validity as an electronic read. The defective card should be marked inoperable and require replacement.
5. Reports and Queries
      * Query access history by person, date, department and type (employee, visitor, patient).
      * Generate department occupancy reports.
      * Generate reports on compliance with scheduled hours and total hours worked for a given period (month, week, day).
      * The system must alert whenever a department reaches 60%, 80% and 100% of its operational capacity.

Remember:
1. Complete STEP 1 - STEP 5 fully before generating JSON
2. Be explicit and thorough in your reasoning
3. Reference the requirement directly when explaining your choices
4. Output ONLY the JSON after your reasoning (no additional commentary)

format_params_used.input_requirements:
ER Modeling Scenario
Integrated Hospital Management System for Multiple Facilities
Business Context
You have been hired to model the database for a hospital network that operates 15 units across 3 Brazilian states. The system must control, record and monitor the entry and exit of all people who access the hospitals, including staff, visitors and patients, ensuring traceability, security and compliance with the institution's internal rules.
Functional Requirements
1. Hospital
Each hospital has departments responsible for specific activities. Not every facility in the network needs to offer all services: each hospital declares the departments, human resources and capacities it has (available teams and departments).
Departments present in the network:
* Emergency
* ICU (Intensive Care Unit)
* Surgical Center / Operating Theaters
* Adult Ward
* Pediatric Ward
* Maternity
* Radiology / Imaging (X-ray, CT, MRI)
* Clinical Laboratory
* Outpatient Clinic
* Hospital Pharmacy
* Social Services
* Triage
* Reception
1.1 Departments
* Each department has an assigned coordinator.
* There must be a leader or person responsible for the department in every shift (there should always be someone accountable for the department at any time).

2. People Management
The hospital receives individuals in multiple categories; some are employees and others are not. Categories include: visitor, patient, physician, nurse, resident physician, researcher and administrative staff. Physicians may work in multiple units under different contract types (for example, CLT, PJ, on-call, daily-rate). Residents are licensed physicians in training who also attend patients under supervision, so it is important to record the supervising physician for each resident. VIP patients have special care rules and enhanced privacy (these are procedural and privacy measures, not related to the medical quality of care). Employees may have dependents who are also patients. The hospital records the following for every person who accesses it: full name, addresses, contact phone numbers, CPF, RG and date of birth. (CPF and RG are Brazilian identifiers: CPF = taxpayer ID, RG = identity card.)
2.1 Patients
   * Each patient receives an identification wristband.
   * The system must record patient movements between departments, including hospital admission, transfers between departments and discharge.
   * A complete history of the departments each patient has passed through must be maintained.
   * The system must indicate whether the patient is awaiting care, in care, admitted (inpatient), under observation, in surgery, or discharged.
2.2 Visitors
   * Not every visitor is accompanying a patient.
   * The type of visitor must be recorded (for example, technical visit, regulatory inspection, etc.).
   * Visitor classification is essential to determine access restrictions (important information for the security team).
   * Each visitor receives an identification card at entry.
   * Not every patient is allowed to have a companion. Valid companion cases include: minors; elderly patients; pregnant patients; women in labor; postpartum patients; patients with prolonged or severe hospitalization, at the discretion of the attending physician or care team; and situations of emotional or cognitive vulnerability when the physician or social services deem it necessary.
   * If patient isolation is required, the physician must document the reason for isolation in a clinical note.
   * The system must record the visitor's exit (mandatory).
   * Access outside visiting hours must be prevented unless special authorization is granted by the hospital.
2.3 Employees
   * Employees must be clearly identified.
   * A researcher can be either a nurse or a physician only; researcher is not an independent occupational category.
   * A resident physician cannot be a coordinator or be responsible for a department, even temporarily.
   * Physicians and nurses may also hold administrative roles (for example, responsible for coordinating a department).
   * Any employee may still be treated by the hospital, be present as a visitor, or accompany a patient.
2.4 Addresses
   * A person may have more than one registered address.
   * Employees update their addresses periodically.
   * When possible, patients and visitors should also have updated addresses.
   * It is important to store the timestamp of the last address update.
   * If the last update was more than six months ago, the system should alert and request a registration update.
2.5 Identification Cards
   * Identification cards have an issue date and an expiry date, recorded in the system for security purposes.
   * Cards have a type that designates access level (visually distinguished by different colors).
   * When a card is in use it is marked as active.
   * Because patients and visitors do not have personalized cards, a card's identifier number may be temporarily linked to the person's record while in use.
   * Only personalized cards (employees) must include a photo.
3. Employee Scheduling
Hospital units require an integrated time and attendance system that covers all 15 units to prevent staff from being accidentally scheduled at two locations at the same time or assigned impractical schedules. Professionals follow different work routines. Administration must precisely control the hours worked by each employee. Different hospitals in the network may have different scheduling contracts with the same employee. It is also necessary to record events that remove an employee from duties, such as days off, medical leave, vacation or training.
3.1 Time Clock Records
   * The system must record each employee's clock-in and clock-out times.
   * As mentioned, each employee has an identification card, which facilitates this task.
   * Time records may generate an alert if an employee exceeds scheduled hours or misses a clock record; this is useful for HR.
3.2 Work Shifts
   * Some employees have more flexible shifts than others.
   * Administrative staff generally have fixed weekdays and start/end times.
   * Nurses may have fixed rules or work rotating shifts.
   * Physicians similarly may have variable schedules and may come to the hospital only to perform consultations, monitoring, surgeries, etc., leaving when those activities are completed.
   * The employment contract governs how working hours are administered by the contracting institution.
   * The key requirement is accurate recording of clock-in and clock-out times for HR verification of compliance with agreed schedules.
3.3 Availability Exceptions
   * Reasons that remove a professional from scheduled activities must be well documented.
   * Each exception should include start and end dates, type (day off, medical leave, vacation, training) and an optional textual note.
3.4 Shift Swaps
   * Shift swaps among health staff are common and permitted.
   * Whenever a swap occurs, it is important to record the involved employees and the coordinator who approved the swap.
   * Each department has its coordinator, who may be a physician, a nurse or an administrative employee.
   * The justification for the swap request should also be recorded (useful for HR metrics about the need for permanent schedule changes, employee health considerations, etc.).
4. Special Situations and Contingency Procedures
4.1 Lost Identification Card
The system must allow immediate registration of a lost card. When a loss is reported:
   * the card is automatically invalidated;
   * a new card may be issued and linked to the person;
   * every access attempt using the lost card is blocked and logged;
   * the event generates an alert for security.
A history of cards (active, replaced, lost, expired) must be maintained.
4.2 Card Failure (Defective or Inoperable Card)
When a card fails to read, the system must record: the person involved; the reason for the failure; the read attempt; and the manual verification performed (security or reception staff responsible). Manual validation of entry/exit using an official document plus employee ID/registration must be allowed. After manual validation, the system records the entry/exit with the same legal validity as an electronic read. The defective card should be marked inoperable and require replacement.
5. Reports and Queries
      * Query access history by person, date, department and type (employee, visitor, patient).
      * Generate department occupancy reports.
      * Generate reports on compliance with scheduled hours and total hours worked for a given period (month, week, day).
      * The system must alert whenever a department reaches 60%, 80% and 100% of its operational capacity.
